audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Samson Timnah marriage, lion and honey, feast, riddle, coercion, and Ashkelon killing narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for intermarriage/uncircumcised rhetoric, ambiguous Divine occasion clause, lion and carcass episodes, wedding-feast companions, riddle wager, threat against Samson's wife, pressure disclosure, heifer metaphor, thirty killings for garments, and transfer of the wife to a companion.

verses.restored: Samson went down to Timnah and saw a woman in Timnah from daughters of Philistines.
He went up and told his father and his mother and said, I saw a woman in Timnah from daughters of Philistines; now take her for me as wife.

His father and his mother said to him, Is there no woman among daughters of your kin or among all my people, that you are going to take a wife from uncircumcised Philistines? Samson said to his father, Take her for me, because she is right in my eyes.
His father and his mother did not know that this was from YHWH, because he was seeking an occasion from Philistines. At that time Philistines were ruling in Israel.
Samson went down, and his father and his mother, to Timnah. They came as far as vineyards of Timnah, and look—a young lion of lions roared toward him.
Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and he tore it apart as one tears apart a young goat, though nothing was in his hand. He did not tell his father or his mother what he had done.
He went down and spoke with the woman, and she was right in eyes of Samson.
After days he returned to take her, and turned aside to see fallen body of lion. Look—a community/swarm of bees was in body of lion, and honey.
He scraped it into his palms and went on, walking and eating. He went to his father and his mother and gave to them, and they ate; he did not tell them that from body of lion he scraped honey.
His father went down to the woman, and Samson made there a feast, because so young men would do.
When they saw him, they took thirty companions, and they were with him.
Samson said to them, Let me please riddle a riddle to you. If telling, you tell it to me in seven days of feast and find it, I will give you thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothing.
But if you are not able to tell me, you shall give me thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothing. They said to him, Riddle your riddle, and let us hear it.
He said to them, From the eater came food, and from strong came sweet. They were not able to tell the riddle for three days.
On seventh day they said to Samson's wife, Entice your husband so he will tell us the riddle, lest we burn you and house of your father with fire. Did you invite us to dispossess/impoverish us?

Samson's wife wept upon him and said, You only hate me and do not love me. You riddled a riddle to sons of my people, but to me you have not told it. He said to her, Look, to my father and my mother I have not told it; and to you should I tell?
She wept upon him seven days during which the feast was theirs. On seventh day he told her because she pressed him, and she told the riddle to sons of her people.

Men of city said to him on seventh day before sun went down, What is sweeter than honey, and what is stronger than lion? He said to them, If you had not plowed with my heifer, you would not have found my riddle.

Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him. He went down to Ashkelon and struck thirty men from them, took their stripped garments, and gave changes to tellers of riddle. His anger burned, and he went up to house of his father.
Samson's wife became wife of his companion who had been his companion.

translation_notes: The woman of Timnah is never named. Samson first sees her, then commands his parents to obtain her; her own desire and consent are not narrated.
The term 'uncircumcised Philistines' is ancient boundary polemic and may not authorize ethnic slurs, anti-intermarriage ideology, xenophobia, or contempt for uncircumcised bodies.

Verse 4's pronouns are ambiguous: YHWH or Samson may be the seeker of an occasion. The claim that the situation is 'from YHWH' does not make coercion, killing, or the treatment of the woman as an instrument morally good.
The lion and honey scene involves animal death, corpse contact, secrecy, and possible Nazirite impurity tension; it is not a model for proving strength.
The thirty companions may be wedding attendants, watchers, or a security presence. The text does not explain why they are assigned.
The riddle wager creates economic pressure, but the companions answer it by threatening to burn the woman and her father's house. Her disclosure occurs under credible threat of lethal violence.
Verse 16 records relational pressure between wife and husband, but it must be read alongside the death threat against her. She is not a simple seductress or traitor.
Samson's 'my heifer' metaphor objectifies his wife and casts other men as using her body/labor. Preserve the metaphor as evidence of patriarchal speech, not acceptable relationship language.
Samson kills thirty men in Ashkelon to pay a clothing debt. The Spirit formula reports narrative empowerment but must not be treated as blanket moral approval of revenge or killing uninvolved people.
The wife is transferred to Samson's companion without her voice. Marriage arrangements in this chapter repeatedly deny her bodily and relational autonomy.
safety_note: Do not blame the Timnite woman for disclosing the riddle under an arson-and-death threat. Reject misogynistic readings that make her a stereotype of deceptive women. Do not use Divine providence language to sanctify forced marriage, ethnic hostility, animal cruelty, coercion, revenge killing, theft, or treating a woman as property.
